g.data-narration: Welcome to Command Line Arguments in C - a powerful way to pass data to programs during execution.
g.text: Learn to pass parameters to programs at execution time

g.data-narration: Command line arguments are parameters passed to a program when executing it from the terminal, enabling non-interactive data input.
g.text: Parameters passed to a program at the time of execution from the OS command line.
Instead of using interactive input like scanf(), data is provided directly during execution.
Used for: configuration, file processing, building CLI utilities

g.data-narration: Command line arguments enable automation, scripting, and building flexible programs that can process variable data without user prompts.
The main function accepts two parameters: argc holds argument count, and argv is an array of character pointers containing the arguments.
g.text: // argc = argument count, argv = argument vector
Both parameters enable CLI input handling

g.data-narration: argc is an integer representing the total number of arguments passed, always including the program name itself as the first argument.
argv is an array of character pointers (strings). argv[0] holds the program name, argv[1] onwards hold user arguments, and argv[argc] is NULL.
g.text: This allows safe iteration and marks the end of arguments

g.data-narration: This diagram shows the complete argv array structure with indices, contents, and the NULL terminator for a sample execution.
Arguments arrive as strings. Use atoi() for integers and atof() for floats to convert string arguments to numeric values for calculations.
g.text: All command line arguments are received as strings (char*). Use conversion functions:

g.data-narration: This basic example prints all command line arguments using a loop, showing how argc and argv work together to access each argument.
This example shows converting string arguments to integers using atoi(), then summing them to produce a numeric result.
g.text: Note: Loop starts from i=1 to skip program name

g.data-narration: File operations commonly use command line arguments for source and destination filenames, enabling powerful file processing utilities.
g.text: Example 3: File Copy Using Command Line Arguments

g.data-narration: Programs use flags like -v or --verbose for options. Parsing argv identifies flags and their associated values for program configuration.
g.text: Programs often use hyphen notation for options: -v (verbose), -n (count), --verbose (long form)

g.data-narration: Remember: argc includes program name, argv[0] is program name, argv[argc] is NULL, and always validate arguments before use.
g.text: Program name counts as first argument

g.data-narration: Avoid these common errors: forgetting argc validation, not converting strings to numbers, and confusing argv indexing.
g.text: Always check argc before accessing argv

g.data-narration: For exams: memorize the main signature, remember argc includes program name, and always validate before accessing argv elements.
g.text: atoi() for int, atof() for float
